The tool query_db was executed successfully. The result is stored under key: var_functions.query_db:16 The result is: [{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}]

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import pandas as pd
from collections import defaultdict

# Load the file paths
books_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:22.json'
reviews_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:20.json'

# Load the books data (English Literature & Fiction books)
books_data = []
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Load the reviews data (ratings >= 4.9)
reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Clean column names
books_df.columns = books_df.columns.str.strip()
reviews_df.columns = reviews_df.columns.str.strip()

# Convert rating to float
reviews_df['rating'] = reviews_df['rating'].astype(float)

# Map purchase_id to book_id (they should match based on hint)
# The hint says book_id in books_info matches purchase_id in review
reviews_df['book_id'] = reviews_df['purchase_id']

# Group reviews by book_id and calculate average rating
book_ratings = reviews_df.groupby('book_id').agg({
    'rating': ['mean', 'count']
}).reset_index()

# Flatten column names
book_ratings.columns = ['book_id', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']

# Round average rating to handle floating point issues
book_ratings['avg_rating'] = book_ratings['avg_rating'].round(2)

# Find books with perfect 5.0 average rating
perfect_ratings = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] == 5.0]

# Merge with books to get titles
good_books = books_df.merge(perfect_ratings, on='book_id', how='inner')

print('__RESULT__:')
result = {
    'total_books': len(books_df),
    'total_reviews': len(reviews_df),
    'books_with_perfect_rating': len(good_books),
    'matching_books': good_books[['book_id', 'title', 'categories', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']].to_dict('records')
}
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

message.reasoning:
I need to find English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category that have a perfect average rating of 5.0.

Let me analyze what I have so far:
1. Books in Literature & Fiction category (from query_db:18)
2. All reviews with rating >= 4.9 (from query_db:20)

The issue is that I need to find books that have an EXACT average rating of 5.0, which means all reviews for that book must be 5.0.

Let me write a more comprehensive query to:
1. First identify all Literature & Fiction books in English
2. Then check their average ratings in the review table
3. Only return those with perfect 5.0 average

I need to:
- Query books_table for Literature & Fiction books in English
- Query review_table for all reviews (not just >= 4.9)
- Calculate average ratings per book
- Filter for average = 5.0
- Join back with books to get titles

Let me load all the review data and book data to analyze in Python.

- execute_python: run Python code.

- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).

{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:
{"tool": "list_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name"}}

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category have a perfect average rating of 5.0? Return all matching books.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

2. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Review Title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_info table in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_info.

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}]

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import pandas as pd
from collections import defaultdict

# Load the file paths
books_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:22.json'
reviews_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:20.json'

# Load the books data (English Literature & Fiction books)
books_data = []
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Load the reviews data (ratings >= 4.9)
reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Clean column names
books_df.columns = books_df.columns.str.strip()
reviews_df.columns = reviews_df.columns.str.strip()

# Convert rating to float
reviews_df['rating'] = reviews_df['rating'].astype(float)

# Map purchase_id to book_id (they should match based on hint)
# The hint says book_id in books_info matches purchase_id in review
reviews_df['book_id'] = reviews_df['purchase_id']

# Group reviews by book_id and calculate average rating
book_ratings = reviews_df.groupby('book_id').agg({
    'rating': ['mean', 'count']
}).reset_index()

# Flatten column names
book_ratings.columns = ['book_id', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']

# Round average rating to handle floating point issues
book_ratings['avg_rating'] = book_ratings['avg_rating'].round(2)

# Find books with perfect 5.0 average rating
perfect_ratings = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] == 5.0]

# Merge with books to get titles
good_books = books_df.merge(perfect_ratings, on='book_id', how='inner')

print('__RESULT__:')
result = {
    'total_books': len(books_df),
    'total_reviews': len(reviews_df),
    'books_with_perfect_rating': len(good_books),
    'matching_books': good_books[['book_id', 'title', 'categories', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']].to_dict('records')
}
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

books_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:30.json'

reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Clean column names
books_df.columns = books_df.columns.str.strip()
reviews_df.columns = reviews_df.columns.str.strip()

# Convert data types
reviews_df['rating'] = reviews_df['rating'].astype(float)

print(f'Total books: {len(books_df)}')
print(f'Total reviews: {len(reviews_df)}')

print('\nSample reviews:')

"}

QUERY:
Which English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category have a perfect average rating of 5.0? Return all matching books.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

2. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Review Title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_info table in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_info.

The tool query_db was executed successfully. The result is stored under key: var_functions.query_db:16 The result is: [{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}]

{"code": "import json
import pandas as pd

# Load the file paths
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:20']

# Load the actual data
books_data = []
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

print(f'Loaded {len(books_data)} books and {len(reviews_data)} reviews')
"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import pandas as pd
from collections import defaultdict

# Load the file paths
books_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:22.json'
reviews_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:20.json'

# Load the books data (English Literature & Fiction books)
books_data = []
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Load the reviews data (ratings >= 4.9)
reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Clean column names
books_df.columns = books_df.columns.str.strip()
reviews_df.columns = reviews_df.columns.str.strip()

# Convert rating to float
reviews_df['rating'] = reviews_df['rating'].astype(float)

# Map purchase_id to book_id (they should match based on hint)
# The hint says book_id in books_info matches purchase_id in review
reviews_df['book_id'] = reviews_df['purchase_id']

# Group reviews by book_id and calculate average rating
book_ratings = reviews_df.groupby('book_id').agg({
    'rating': ['mean', 'count']
}).reset_index()

# Flatten column names
book_ratings.columns = ['book_id', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']

# Round average rating to handle floating point issues
book_ratings['avg_rating'] = book_ratings['avg_rating'].round(2)

# Find books with perfect 5.0 average rating
perfect_ratings = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] == 5.0]

# Merge with books to get titles
good_books = books_df.merge(perfect_ratings, on='book_id', how='inner')

print('__RESULT__:')
result = {
    'total_books': len(books_df),
    'total_reviews': len(reviews_df),
    'books_with_perfect_rating': len(good_books),
    'matching_books': good_books[['book_id', 'title', 'categories', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']].to_dict('records')
}
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import pandas as pd

# Load all books data
books_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:30.json'
reviews_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:32.json'

books_data = []
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Convert rating to numeric
reviews_df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(reviews_df['rating'], errors='coerce')

# Map purchase_id to book_id format for joining
reviews_df['book_id'] = reviews_df['purchase_id']

# Filter for English books in Literature & Fiction category
fic_books = []
for _, book in books_df.iterrows():
    # Check if it's Literature & Fiction
    if book['categories'] and 'Literature & Fiction' in str(book['categories']):
        # Check if it's English
        if book['details'] and 'English' in str(book['details']):
            fic_books.append(book.to_dict())

fic_books_df = pd.DataFrame(fic_books)
print(f'Found {len(fic_books_df)} English Literature & Fiction books')

# Calculate average ratings
book_ratings = reviews_df.groupby('book_id').agg({
    'rating': ['mean', 'count']
}).round(2)

book_ratings.columns = ['avg_rating', 'review_count']
book_ratings = book_ratings.reset_index()

# Find books with perfect 5.0 rating
perfect_books = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] == 5.0]
print(f'Found {len(perfect_books)} books with perfect 5.0 rating')

# Merge with Literature & Fiction books
result = fic_books_df.merge(perfect_books, on='book_id', how='inner')

print('__RESULT__:')
output = {
    'count': len(result),
    'books': []
}

if len(result) > 0:
    for _, row in result.iterrows():
        output['books'].append({
            'title': row['title'],
            'book_id': row['book_id'],
            'categories': row['categories'],
            'avg_rating': float(row['avg_rating']),
            'review_count': int(row['review_count'])
        })

print(json.dumps(output, indent=2))"}
function.name: execute_python

message.reasoning:
The user wants to find English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category with a perfect average rating of 5.0. Let me analyze the data structure:

1. **Books database (PostgreSQL)**: 
   - Table: `books_info`
   - Fields: `book_id`, `title`, `categories` (stored as string representation of list/dict), `details` (contains language info)
   - Need to filter for 'Literature & Fiction' in categories and English language in details

2. **Reviews database (SQLite)**:
   - Table: `review`
   - Fields: `purchase_id` (matches `book_id` in books_info), `rating`
   - Need to calculate average rating per book and find those with exactly 5.0

3. **Key issues**:
   - `categories` is stored as a string like `["Books", "Literature & Fiction", "History & Criticism"]`
   - Need to join on `book_id` (books_info) = `purchase_id` (review)
   - Need to filter for English language in `details` field
   - Need to calculate average rating and find perfect 5.0

Let me now write the correct approach using execute_python to process the data properly.

messages.content:
You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):
- query_db: run a SQL or Mongo query. Returns a list of JSON-serializable records or an error string.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>", "query": "<SQL or Mongo query>"}
- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

QUERY:
Which English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category have a perfect average rating of 5.0? Return all matching books.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

2. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Review Title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_info table in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_info.

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}]

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import pandas as pd
from collections import defaultdict

# Load the file paths
books_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:22.json'
reviews_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:20.json'

# Load the books data (English Literature & Fiction books)
books_data = []
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Load the reviews data (ratings >= 4.9)
reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Clean column names
books_df.columns = books_df.columns.str.strip()
reviews_df.columns = reviews_df.columns.str.strip()

# Convert rating to float
reviews_df['rating'] = reviews_df['rating'].astype(float)

# Map purchase_id to book_id (they should match based on hint)
# The hint says book_id in books_info matches purchase_id in review
reviews_df['book_id'] = reviews_df['purchase_id']

# Group reviews by book_id and calculate average rating
book_ratings = reviews_df.groupby('book_id').agg({
    'rating': ['mean', 'count']
}).reset_index()

# Flatten column names
book_ratings.columns = ['book_id', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']

# Round average rating to handle floating point issues
book_ratings['avg_rating'] = book_ratings['avg_rating'].round(2)

# Find books with perfect 5.0 average rating
perfect_ratings = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] == 5.0]

# Merge with books to get titles
good_books = books_df.merge(perfect_ratings, on='book_id', how='inner')

print('__RESULT__:')
result = {
    'total_books': len(books_df),
    'total_reviews': len(reviews_df),
    'books_with_perfect_rating': len(good_books),
    'matching_books': good_books[['book_id', 'title', 'categories', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']].to_dict('records')
}
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

import pandas as pd

# Open the files
books_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:30.json'
reviews_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:32.json'

books_data = []
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Clean column names
books_df.columns = books_df.columns.str.strip()
reviews_df.columns = reviews_df.columns.str.strip()

# Convert data types
reviews_df['rating'] = reviews_df['rating'].astype(float)

print(f'Total books: {len(books_df)}')
print(f'Total reviews: {len(reviews_df)}')
print(f'Books columns: {books_df.columns.tolist()}')
print(f'Reviews columns: {reviews_df.columns.tolist()}')

# Check a few books
print('\nSample books:')
print(books_df[['book_id', 'title', 'categories']].head())

# Check categories content more carefully
print('\nCategories for first 5 books:')
for i, row in books_df.head().iterrows():
    print(f"{row['title']}: {row['categories']}")

print('\nSample reviews:')
print(reviews_df.head())
"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import pandas as pd

# Load all data
books_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:30.json'
reviews_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:32.json'

books_data = []
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Create DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Convert rating to numeric
reviews_df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(reviews_df['rating'], errors='coerce')

# Add book_id column to reviews for merging
reviews_df['book_id'] = reviews_df['purchase_id']

# Filter for English Literature & Fiction books
english_fiction_books = []
for _, book in books_df.iterrows():
    categories = str(book.get('categories', ''))
    details = str(book.get('details', ''))
    
    # Check if Literature & Fiction is in categories
    if 'Literature & Fiction' in categories:
        # Check if it's English language
        if 'English' in details:
            english_fiction_books.append(book.to_dict())

fiction_df = pd.DataFrame(english_fiction_books)
print(f'Found {len(fiction_df)} English Literature & Fiction books')

# Calculate average rating for each book
book_stats = reviews_df.groupby('book_id').agg({
    'rating': ['mean', 'count', 'min', 'max']
}).round(2)

book_stats.columns = ['avg_rating', 'review_count', 'min_rating', 'max_rating']
book_stats = book_stats.reset_index()

# Find books with perfect 5.0 average (exactly 5.0, not just close)
perfect_books = book_stats[book_stats['avg_rating'] == 5.0]

print(f'Books with perfect 5.0 rating: {len(perfect_books)}')

# Merge with our English fiction books
final_results = fiction_df.merge(perfect_books, on='book_id', how='inner')

print(f'English Literature & Fiction books with perfect 5.0 rating: {len(final_results)}')

# If none found, let's check if any have all 5-star ratings (even if average rounds to 5.0)
# Also check for books with min_rating = 5.0 (all ratings are 5)
all_five_stars = book_stats[book_stats['min_rating'] == 5.0]
all_five_results = fiction_df.merge(all_five_stars, on='book_id', how='inner')

print(f'English Literature & Fiction books with all 5-star ratings: {len(all_five_results)}')

# Output results
print('__RESULT__:')
output = {
    'perfect_rating_books': [],
    'all_five_star_books': []
}

if len(final_results) > 0:
    for _, row in final_results.iterrows():
        output['perfect_rating_books'].append({
            'title': row['title'],
            'book_id': row['book_id'],
            'categories': row['categories'],
            'avg_rating': float(row['avg_rating']),
            'review_count': int(row['review_count'])
        })

if len(all_five_results) > 0:
    for _, row in all_five_results.iterrows():
        output['all_five_star_books'].append({
            'title': row['title'],
            'book_id': row['book_id'],
            'categories': row['categories'],
            'avg_rating': float(row['avg_rating']),
            'review_count': int(row['review_count'])
        })

print(json.dumps(output, indent=2))"}

message.reasoning:
The user asks: "Which English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category have a perfect average rating of 5.0? Return all matching books."

From my analysis so far, I've found that there are English Literature & Fiction books in the database, but I need to check if any of them have a perfect average rating of 5.0.

My previous query looked for books with rating >= 4.9, but I need to calculate the actual average rating for each book. Let me think about this:

1. I've already filtered for English books in Literature & Fiction category
2. I have all reviews with their ratings
3. I need to calculate the average rating per book
4. I need to find which books have exactly 5.0 average rating

Let me run a more targeted query. I should:
1. Get all reviews to calculate averages
2. Get all English Literature & Fiction books
3. Calculate the average rating for each book
4. Filter for those with exactly 5.0

Actually, let me reconsider. The field "rating_number" in books_info might represent the total number of ratings, not the average. I need to check this. Looking back at the schema:
- rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received

So this is the count, not the average. I need to calculate the average from the review table.

Let me try a different approach: I'll query for books that have all 5-star ratings (meaning no ratings below 5). But first, let me check if any books in the Literature & Fiction category have an average rating of exactly 5.0.

You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):
- query_db: run a SQL or Mongo query. Returns a list of JSON-serializable records or an error string.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>", "query": "<SQL or Mongo query>"}
- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

EXAMPLES:
- query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:
{"tool": "list_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name"}}

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category have a perfect average rating of 5.0? Return all matching books.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

2. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Review Title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_info table in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_info.

The tool query_db was executed successfully. The result is stored under key: var_functions.query_db:16 The result is: [{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}]

{"code": "import json
import pandas as pd

# Load the file paths
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:20']

# Load the actual data
books_data = []
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

print(f'Loaded {len(books_data)} books and {len(reviews_data)} reviews')
"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import pandas as pd
from collections import defaultdict

# Load the file paths
books_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:22.json'
reviews_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:20.json'

# Load the books data (English Literature & Fiction books)
books_data = []
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Load the reviews data (ratings >= 4.9)
reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Clean column names
books_df.columns = books_df.columns.str.strip()
reviews_df.columns = reviews_df.columns.str.strip()

# Convert rating to float
reviews_df['rating'] = reviews_df['rating'].astype(float)

# Map purchase_id to book_id (they should match based on hint)
# The hint says book_id in books_info matches purchase_id in review
reviews_df['book_id'] = reviews_df['purchase_id']

# Group reviews by book_id and calculate average rating
book_ratings = reviews_df.groupby('book_id').agg({
    'rating': ['mean', 'count']
}).reset_index()

# Flatten column names
book_ratings.columns = ['book_id', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']

# Round average rating to handle floating point issues
book_ratings['avg_rating'] = book_ratings['avg_rating'].round(2)

# Find books with perfect 5.0 average rating
perfect_ratings = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] == 5.0]

# Merge with books to get titles
good_books = books_df.merge(perfect_ratings, on='book_id', how='inner')

print('__RESULT__:')
result = {
    'total_books': len(books_df),
    'total_reviews': len(reviews_df),
    'books_with_perfect_rating': len(good_books),
    'matching_books': good_books[['book_id', 'title', 'categories', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']].to_dict('records')
}
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import pandas as pd

# Open the files
books_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:30.json'
reviews_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:32.json'

books_data = []
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Clean column names
books_df.columns = books_df.columns.str.strip()
reviews_df.columns = reviews_df.columns.str.strip()

# Convert data types
reviews_df['rating'] = reviews_df['rating'].astype(float)

print(f'Total books: {len(books_df)}')
print(f'Total reviews: {len(reviews_df)}')
print(f'Books columns: {books_df.columns.tolist()}')
print(f'Reviews columns: {reviews_df.columns.tolist()}')

# Check a few books
print('\nSample books:')
print(books_df[['book_id', 'title', 'categories']].head())

# Check categories content more carefully
print('\nCategories for first 5 books:')
for i, row in books_df.head().iterrows():
    print(f"{row['title']}: {row['categories']}")

print('\nSample reviews:')
print(reviews_df.head())
"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import pandas as pd

# Load all books data
books_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:30.json'
reviews_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:32.json'

books_data = []
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Convert rating to numeric
reviews_df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(reviews_df['rating'], errors='coerce')

# Map purchase_id to book_id format for joining
reviews_df['book_id'] = reviews_df['purchase_id']

# Filter for English books in Literature & Fiction category
fic_books = []
for _, book in books_df.iterrows():
    # Check if it's Literature & Fiction
    if book['categories'] and 'Literature & Fiction' in str(book['categories']):
        # Check if it's English
        if book['details'] and 'English' in str(book['details']):
            fic_books.append(book.to_dict())

fic_books_df = pd.DataFrame(fic_books)
print(f'Found {len(fic_books_df)} English Literature & Fiction books')

# Calculate average ratings
book_ratings = reviews_df.groupby('book_id').agg({
    'rating': ['mean', 'count']
}).round(2)

book_ratings.columns = ['avg_rating', 'review_count']
book_ratings = book_ratings.reset_index()

# Find books with perfect 5.0 rating
perfect_books = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] == 5.0]
print(f'Found {len(perfect_books)} books with perfect 5.0 rating')

# Merge with Literature & Fiction books
result = fic_books_df.merge(perfect_books, on='book_id', how='inner')

print('__RESULT__:')
output = {
    'count': len(result),
    'books': []
}

if len(result) > 0:
    for _, row in result.iterrows():
        output['books'].append({
            'title': row['title'],
            'book_id': row['book_id'],
            'categories': row['categories'],
            'avg_rating': float(row['avg_rating']),
            'review_count': int(row['review_count'])
        })

print(json.dumps(output, indent=2))"}

- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----

print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

- query_db:

QUERY:
Which English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category have a perfect average rating of 5.0? Return all matching books.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

2. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Review Title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_info table in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_info.

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}]

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import pandas as pd
from collections import defaultdict

# Load the file paths
books_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:22.json'
reviews_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:20.json'

# Load the books data (English Literature & Fiction books)
books_data = []
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Load the reviews data (ratings >= 4.9)
reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Clean column names
books_df.columns = books_df.columns.str.strip()
reviews_df.columns = reviews_df.columns.str.strip()

# Convert rating to float
reviews_df['rating'] = reviews_df['rating'].astype(float)

# Map purchase_id to book_id (they should match based on hint)
# The hint says book_id in books_info matches purchase_id in review
reviews_df['book_id'] = reviews_df['purchase_id']

# Group reviews by book_id and calculate average rating
book_ratings = reviews_df.groupby('book_id').agg({
    'rating': ['mean', 'count']
}).reset_index()

# Flatten column names
book_ratings.columns = ['book_id', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']

# Round average rating to handle floating point issues
book_ratings['avg_rating'] = book_ratings['avg_rating'].round(2)

# Find books with perfect 5.0 average rating
perfect_ratings = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] == 5.0]

# Merge with books to get titles
good_books = books_df.merge(perfect_ratings, on='book_id', how='inner')

print('__RESULT__:')
result = {
    'total_books': len(books_df),
    'total_reviews': len(reviews_df),
    'books_with_perfect_rating': len(good_books),
    'matching_books': good_books[['book_id', 'title', 'categories', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']].to_dict('records')
}
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

import pandas as pd

books_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:30.json'
reviews_file_path = 'file_storage/functions.query_db:32.json'

books_data = []
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:

reviews_data = []
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Clean column names
books_df.columns = books_df.columns.str.strip()
reviews_df.columns = reviews_df.columns.str.strip()

# Convert data types
reviews_df['rating'] = reviews_df['rating'].astype(float)

print(f'Total books: {len(books_df)}')
print(f'Total reviews: {len(reviews_df)}')

# Check categories content more carefully

for i, row in books_df.head().iterrows():
    print(f"{row['title']}: {row['categories']}")

print('\nSample reviews:')
print(reviews_df.head())
"}
